title: Balikatan 2026 Officially Opens: Gen. Brawner Declares Exercise Open at Camp Aguinaldo; Japan Fires Type 88 Anti-Ship Missiles in Historic SINKEX
detail: Armed Forces of the Philippines Chief of Staff General Romeo Brawner Jr. officially opened Exercise Balikatan 41-2026 at Camp General Emilio Aguinaldo, Quezon City on April 20, declaring: 'I hereby declare Exercise Balikatan 41-2026 officially open starting today. Let this exercise not only demonstrate our readiness but affirm our commitment to stand together, to act together, and when necessary, defend together.' The 41st and largest-ever iteration involves 17,000+ troops from seven nations — Philippines, United States, Australia, Japan, Canada, France, and New Zealand — with 18 observer nations, running April 20 through May 8. AFP spokesperson Colonel Francel Margareth Padilla described the drills as marking 'a significant shift from traditional drills to joint all-domain operations.' Japan's Defense Minister Shinjiro Koizumi traveled to the Philippines to observe the historic exercise, in which Japan is deploying approximately 1,400 JSDF troops carrying weapons — the first combat-capable Japanese overseas deployment since World War II. A centerpiece of Japan's participation is the Type 88 Surface-to-Ship Missile (SSM-1) exercise, including a planned SINKEX (ship-sinking exercise) against decommissioned Philippine Navy vessel BRP Quezon (PS-70), executed by coordinated Japanese, Philippine, and US forces. The Type 88 SSM has an operational range of approximately 150 km with active radar homing terminal guidance. JS Ise (helicopter destroyer), JS Shimokita (landing ship), and JDS Ikazuchi (destroyer) are among Japan's naval contribution. Small activist protests by organizations including Bayan and the League of Filipino Students were held at Camp Aguinaldo around the opening ceremony, raising concerns over sovereignty and community impact. INDOPACOM and AFP leadership described the exercise as 'the correct path forward' for Philippine defense posture amid escalating Chinese gray-zone operations.

media.caption: AFP Chief General Romeo Brawner Jr. officially opens Exercise Balikatan 41-2026 at Camp Aguinaldo on April 20, 2026 — the largest-ever iteration with 17,000+ troops from 7 nations
Balikatan 2026 opening ceremony at Camp Aguinaldo featuring joint all-domain operations with Japan's historic first WWII-era combat deployment

title: China Warns US, Japan, Philippines Against 'Playing with Fire' Over Balikatan 2026; Beijing Simultaneously Deploys Warships for Western Pacific Drills
detail: Chinese Foreign Ministry spokesperson Guo Jiakun issued Beijing's sharpest public warning over Balikatan 2026 on April 20, 2026 — the exercise's opening day — stating: 'Blindly binding themselves together in the name of security will only be akin to playing with fire — ultimately backfiring upon themselves.' Guo characterized the trilateral US-Japan-Philippines exercise as 'unilateralism and military bullying,' accusing extra-regional powers of 'sowing division and confrontation' and warning that 'regional peace and stability must not be undermined.' China expressed particular concern over the exercises' proximity to Taiwan and disputed South China Sea areas, and separately protested Japan's warship transit through the Taiwan Strait ahead of the exercise. Beijing also deployed its own warships for simultaneous Western Pacific drills — assessed by analysts as a counter-signaling move launched the day after Balikatan opened. However, a professor at Shanghai University of Political Science and Law told the South China Morning Post that China is 'currently seeking to avoid escalation because placing a strategic focus on the Middle East is a diplomatic priority' and 'will not overreact to moves by Japan.' Japan's Ambassador to the Philippines responded on April 20 that the Balikatan exercise was 'not targeting China' and was a defensive multilateral activity consistent with international law. Japan's Defense Minister Shinjiro Koizumi, present in Manila to observe Balikatan 2026, characterized Japan's participation as 'contributing to regional peace and stability through alliance interoperability.' China's simultaneous deployment of warships for Pacific drills — while objecting to allied exercises — was noted by analysts as emblematic of Beijing's asymmetric approach: deploying forces while condemning others for doing the same.

media.caption: China warns US-Japan-Philippines Balikatan 2026 risks regional trust; Beijing deploys warships for simultaneous Pacific drills on opening day
